# crimes — codebase risk workflow

`crimes` is a deterministic CLI (no LLM) that reports change risk and
agent risk. JSON output is the stable contract; prefer it when planning.

## When to run it

- Before editing an unfamiliar file: `crimes context <file> --format json`
- Before a broad refactor: `crimes scan <path> --format json`
- After edits: `crimes scan --changed --format json`
- Before merging a branch: `crimes verdict --format json`

## Decision rules

- Treat any new `severity: "high"` finding introduced by your edit as a blocker unless the user explicitly accepts it.
- Read `evidence[]` before acting; it contains deterministic facts, not LLM opinion.
- Use `scores.agent_risk` to decide which findings need human attention first.
- If a finding is a false positive, record feedback with `crimes feedback <fingerprint> --verdict fp --note "<why>"` rather than silently ignoring it.

Return Shape Roulette — `src/esbuild/svelte.ts` returning unrelated shapes\\\\n\\\\n| ID | Symbol | Lines | Shapes | Example keys |\\\\n|---|---|---|---|---|\\\\n| crime_00054 | `sveltePlugin` | 9–124 | 6, 0% min overlap | `{ args, namespace, path }` vs `{ location, text }` |\\\\n| crime_00055 | `setup` (class method) | 17–122 | 6, 0% min overlap | same |\\\\n| crime_00062 | `<anonymous>` callback | 31–121 | 5, 0% min overlap | `{ location, text }` vs `{ code }` |\\\\n\\\\nThese three are the same function nest: `sveltePlugin` is the factory, `setup` is its body, and the anonymous arrow is the `onLoad` / `onResolve` callback inside `setup`. The 6 shapes are esbuild's plugin protocol — different callbacks return different result types (resolved path vs load result vs error), but the file has no explicit return annotations, so TS infers a union.\\\\n\\\\n**What an agent gets wrong today:** the inferred union widens silently each time you add a branch. An agent adding a new error path that returns `{ message }` instead of `{ text }` won't get a TS error at the return site — they'll only see it when esbuild fails to display the diagnostic at runtime. Worse, the `{ location, text }` shape is esbuild's `PartialMessage` and `{ args, namespace, path }` is `OnResolveResult`; mixing them in one inferred return type means the agent has no signal which callback they're inside.\\\\n\\\\n**Proposal:**\\\\n- Annotate each callback with its esbuild type explicitly: the `onResolve` arrow returns `OnResolveResult | null | undefined`, the `onLoad` arrow returns `OnLoadResult | null | undefined`. These types ship with `esbuild`.\\\\n- Annotate `setup`'s return as `void` (esbuild plugins' `setup` is void-returning by contract).\\\\n- Annotate `sveltePlugin`'s return as `Plugin` from esbuild.\\\\n\\\\nNo structural refactor needed — this is purely \\\\\\\"type the seams\\\\\\\". After this, the inferred-union noise disappears and the detector goes quiet.\\\\n\\\\n## Ordering and risk\\\\n\\\\n1. **First**: the svelte.ts type annotations (lowest risk — pure additions, can ship as one commit).\\\\n2. **Second**: `getRollupConfig → resolveRollupConfig` + `RollupConfigInputs` (one file, one signature, callers compile-checked).\\\\n3. **Third**: extract `prepareRollupSession` and rename `build → runBuild` (touches `src/index.ts`'s `build` orchestrator and `src/rollup.ts`'s `buildStart`; needs a coordinated commit because the side effects move).\\\\n\\\\nNone of these require touching the god-function structure, the `Date.now()` calls, or the test gaps that the larger scan flagged — those are separate workstreams. `generateInvoice`\\\\n\\\\nThe smoking gun is **crime_00004** combined with **crime_00001**:\\\\n\\\\n- `src/billing.ts` calls `Date.now()` / `new Date()` **7 times** at lines 57, 63, 92, 198, 238, 250, 273.\\\\n- All seven calls live inside `generateInvoice` (lines 50–253) or immediately after it.\\\\n- Each call independently observes wall-clock time.\\\\n\\\\nThat pattern is exactly the shape of date-boundary flakiness: a single invoice generation reads the clock multiple times. If the test runs near midnight / month-end / year-end / DST, two reads inside the *same* call can land on different days, months, or years — so `period_start`, `period_end`, `due_date`, the invoice number suffix, etc. disagree with each other. The God Function size (204 lines) hides this from any agent editing one section, which is why the bug survives review.\\\\n\\\\nSupporting evidence the scan surfaces:\\\\n- `crime_00001` (God Function): 3.4× threshold, multiple responsibilities folded together — encourages the \\\\\\\"read the clock again when I need it\\\\\\\" pattern.\\\\n- `crime_00006` and `crime_00016` (weak tests in `billing.test.ts`): one test has zero asserts, the other only weak matchers — so the test suite never pins time, never asserts boundary behaviour, and the flake only shows up when the runner happens to straddle a boundary.\\\\n\\\\n## Proposed fix\\\\n\\\\n1. **Inject a clock.** Add a `now: Date` (or `clock: { now(): Date }`) parameter to `generateInvoice`. Call it **once** at the top of the function and pass the resulting `Date` (or epoch ms) to every downstream computation. False Identity (`name_behavior_mismatch`)\\\\n\\\\n| Finding | Symbol | Side-effect evidence |\\\\n|---|---|---|\\\\n| crime_00032 | `src/index.ts:build` (167–459) | `createLogger`, `removeFiles`, 4 side-effect signals |\\\\n| crime_00054 | `src/index.ts:buildAll` (291–365) | `removeFiles`, 3 side-effect signals |\\\\n| crime_00049 | `src/rollup.ts:getRollupConfig` (38–159) | `setSilent`, `removeFiles`, 4 side-effect signals |\\\\n| crime_00072 | `src/rollup.ts:buildStart` (77–81) | `removeFiles`, 2 side-effect signals |\\\\n\\\\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** All four names imply pure reads (`build` reads like a noun-builder/factory, `getRollupConfig` reads like a getter, `buildStart` reads like a lifecycle no-op). An agent extending this code is likely to:\\\\n\\\\n- Memoize `getRollupConfig` to avoid \\\\\\\"recomputing config\\\\\\\" — silently dropping the `clean`/`setSilent` calls on cache hits.\\\\n- Call `build()` twice in a watcher loop (e.g. \\\\\\\"compute first, run later\\\\\\\") — deleting files twice.\\\\n- Wrap `buildStart` in a debounce — turning a clean-before-build into an intermittent clean.\\\\n\\\\n**Proposal.**\\\\n\\\\n- **`build` → `runBuild`.** Extract a pure `resolveBuildPlan(options): BuildPlan` step; have `runBuild` accept that plan and own the logger/`removeFiles` side effects. This also cuts the 293-line God Function (crime_00003) into separable units.\\\\n- **`buildAll` → `runAllBuilds`.** Hoist the `removeFiles` call to the caller (or guard it behind a one-shot flag) so the per-build function is genuinely side-effect-free.\\\\n- **`getRollupConfig` → split.** Rename to `buildRollupConfig` (or `prepareRollupBuild`) and extract two helpers: `applyLogLevel(opts)` and `cleanOutDirs(opts)`. The function is currently a getter that destroys filesystem state — that's the heart of the agent risk.\\\\n- **`buildStart`.** The name is fixed by Rollup's plugin contract, so don't rename. Instead, gate the `removeFiles` call behind `if (options.clean)` and add a one-line comment naming the side effect — that's the minimum disclosure.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 2. Option Bag Junk Drawer (`option_bag_junk_drawer`)\\\\n\\\\n| Finding | Symbol | Property reads |\\\\n|---|---|---|\\\\n| crime_00053 | `src/rollup.ts:getRollupConfig` | 10: `cjsInterop, clean, dts, entry, external, format, outDir, outExtension, …` |\\\\n\\\\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** `getRollupConfig(options)` accepts the full `NormalizedOptions` and pulls 10 fields out of it implicitly. An agent that:\\\\n\\\\n- Adds a new option (`outDirs` plural, `dtsEntry` separate) won't know whether to wire it here — the function reads the bag opaquely.\\\\n- Removes a field they grep-confirmed is \\\\\\\"only read in `index.ts`\\\\\\\" can silently break this codepath because the property access is hidden inside method bodies.\\\\n- Tries to reuse `getRollupConfig` from a test by passing a `{}` literal — TypeScript accepts it (the param is the wide `NormalizedOptions`), runtime fails on the first missing field.\\\\n\\\\n**Proposal.** Replace `options: NormalizedOptions` with an explicit, narrower shape:\\\\n\\\\n```ts\\\\ntype RollupConfigInputs = Pick<\\\\n  NormalizedOptions,\\\\n  'cjsInterop' | 'clean' | 'dts' | 'entry' | 'external'\\\\n  | 'format' | 'outDir' | 'outExtension' | 'silent' | /* …the remaining 1 */\\\\n>;\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nEven better: destructure at the call site so each helper takes only what it needs. That converts the agent's question from \\\\\\\"does this function use field X?\\\\\\\" to a type error.\\\\n\\\\nThe same pattern applies to crime_00050 (`runEsbuild` reads 38 distinct properties) and crime_00056/57 (`main` reads 8) — the user scoped the question to the three named files, but the same `Pick<>`/destructure recipe lifts straight over.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 3. Return Shape Roulette (`return_shape_roulette`)\\\\n\\\\n| Finding | Symbol | Shapes | Example divergence |\\\\n|---|---|---|---|\\\\n| crime_00051 | `src/esbuild/svelte.ts:sveltePlugin` (9–124) | 6, 0% overlap | `{ args, namespace, path }` vs `{ location, text }` |\\\\n| crime_00052 | `src/esbuild/svelte.ts:setup` (17–122) | 6, 0% overlap | same — `setup` is the inner callback of `sveltePlugin` |\\\\n| crime_00055 | `src/esbuild/svelte.ts:<anonymous>` (31–121) | 5, 0% overlap | `{ location, text }` vs `{ code }` |\\\\n\\\\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** Without an annotated return type, TS infers the union `{args,namespace,path} | {location,text} | {code} | …`. The shapes are esbuild plugin callbacks (`onResolve` → `OnResolveResult`, `onLoad` → `OnLoadResult`, errors → `PartialMessage`, transform → `{ code }`), but the file doesn't *say* that. An agent who:\\\\n\\\\n- Adds an error path returning `{ message: 'svelte preprocess failed' }` gets no TS error — esbuild silently ignores the unknown shape and the diagnostic disappears.\\\\n- Refactors one branch into a helper and lifts the inferred return type as the helper's signature — now every caller pays for the union and the next agent assumes all branches return all fields.\\\\n- Tries to add a `pluginData` field to the resolve result by spreading it onto the error-path return.\\\\n\\\\n**Proposal.** Annotate each callback explicitly with esbuild's exported types:\\\\n\\\\n```ts\\\\nimport type {\\\\n  OnResolveResult, OnLoadResult, PartialMessage,\\\\n} from 'esbuild';\\\\n\\\\nbuild.onResolve({ filter }, async (args): Promise<OnResolveResult | undefined> => { … });\\\\nbuild.onLoad({ filter }, async (args): Promise<OnLoadResult> => { … });\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nThat single change converts all three findings on this file: future divergent shapes fail at the type checker instead of at runtime, and an agent reading the file sees the contract without having to infer it from six return statements.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## Order of operations\\\\n\\\\n1. **Group 3 first (svelte.ts annotations).** Pure type additions, zero behavior change, removes three findings.\\\\n2. **Group 1 renames + side-effect extraction.** Touches more files but each rename is mechanical; the `getRollupConfig` split also chips at the God Function in crime_00008.\\\\n3. **Group 2 (`RollupConfigInputs`).** Do last — narrowing the type may surface bugs in the call sites that the prior two steps haven't already touched.\\\\n\\\\nNone of these are load-bearing in the high_fan_in_fan_out sense for the three target files (`rollup.ts` fan-in 0, `svelte.ts` not in the connectivity tier, `index.ts` is fan-in 4 but the renames are internal). It almost certainly bundles several responsibilities (the file also carries 7 `Date.now()`/`new Date()` calls per `crime_00009`, scattered across lines 57–273, which suggests time-handling, line-item math, and side effects are interleaved). An agent editing the proration section can silently break the tax/rounding/email section because nothing in the function structure tells it where the seams are. Extract cohesive sections — `buildLineItems`, `applyProration`, `finalizeInvoice` — so each section can be read in isolation and tested without spinning up the whole pipeline. This also shrinks the file under the 310-line `crime_00016` threshold as a side effect.\\\\n\\\\n## 2. Rename or split `calculateRefundPreview` (lines 40–44)\\\\n**Tied to:** `crime_00013` — False Identity, plus the orphan rule in `crime_00017`.\\\\n\\\\nThe name says \\\\\\\"preview\\\\\\\" (read-only, safe to call from a UI handler), but the body calls `saveRefundAudit` and `sendRefundEmail` — and the comment on line 39 (\\\\\\\"Only owners can refund enterprise plans unless billing support approves\\\\\\\") encodes a policy that isn't visible in the call signature. This is the highest-leverage agent risk in the file: an agent wiring up a refund confirmation modal will reasonably call `calculateRefundPreview` to populate the preview number and unintentionally email the customer and write an audit row. Split into a pure `calculateRefundAmount(...)` plus an explicit `commitRefund(...)` that owns the side effects, and lift the comment's rule into a typed `canRefund(actor, plan)` guard so it can't be missed.\\\\n\\\\n## 3. Inject a clock to retire the 7 direct `Date` calls\\\\n**Tied to:** `crime_00009` — Temporal Recklessness in billing.ts, reinforced by `crime_00011` and `crime_00030` showing the existing billing tests assert nothing.\\\\n\\\\nSeven `Date.now()` / `new Date()` calls in invoice generation means proration windows, due-date math, and audit timestamps all read the wall clock at call time. With the test suite already weak (one zero-assert test, one with only weak matchers), there's no safety net to catch a regression introduced when you do (1) and (2). Threading a `clock: () => Date` parameter (or a module-level injected `now()`) through `generateInvoice` is cheap, unblocks meaningful tests on the extracted helpers, and lets you delete the cross-cutting `crime_00009` finding in one pass rather than chasing each `Date.now()` individually after the split.\\\\n\\\\n**Order matters:** do (3) before (1) so the extracted helpers can be tested deterministically as you pull them out, and do (2) standalone — it doesn't depend on the split and the misleading-name risk is too sharp to leave sitting while you refactor around /Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.15/codex/bugfix-01-messy-ts-app.json:7:  "response": "{\"type\":\"thread.started\",\"thread_id\":\"019e3cc8-03cb-7692-a072-3f69ab40a16d\"}\n{\"type\":\"turn.started\"}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_0\",\"type\":\"agent_message\",\"text\":\"Most likely cause: billing code reads wall time directly and probably crosses a date boundary during the test.\\n\\nThe strongest findings are:\\n\\n- `src/billing.ts` has 7 direct `Date.now()` / `new Date()` calls, including inside `generateInvoice`.\\n- `src/date.ts` mixes UTC and local methods on the same `Date` receiver: `getUTCFullYear()` with `getMonth()`.\\n- `src/date.ts` also has timezone-unsafe parses and DST-naive day math.\\n\\nFor a date-boundary flaky billing test, the likely failure mode is: invoice generation observes “now” multiple times, or formats/computes billing dates partly in UTC and partly in local time. Around midnight, DST, or when CI runs in a different timezone, the invoice period/due date can shift by one day or month.\\n\\nProposed fix:\\n\\n1. Inject a clock into billing code instead of calling `Date.now()` / `new Date()` directly.\\n2. Capture `now` once at the top of `generateInvoice`.\\n3. Use one date family consistently, preferably UTC for billing/domain logic.\\n4. Add a regression test pinned to a boundary instant, for example `2026-03-01T00:00:00.000Z` or a DST transition case.\\n\\nExample shape:\\n\\n```ts\\ntype Clock = {\\n  now(): Date;\\n};\\n\\nconst systemClock: Clock = {\\n  now: () => new Date(),\\n};\\n\\nexport function generateInvoice(input: InvoiceInput, clock: Clock = systemClock) {\\n  const now = clock.now();\\n\\n  // derive all billing dates from `now`\\n  // use getUTCFullYear(), getUTCMonth(), getUTCDate(), etc.\\n}\\n```\\n\\nThen in tests:\\n\\n```ts\\nconst fixedClock = {\\n  now: () => new Date(\\\"2026-03-01T00:00:00.000Z\\\"),\\n};\\n\\nconst invoice = generateInvoice(input, fixedClock);\\n\\nexpect(invoice.periodStart).toBe(\\\"2026-03-01\\\");\\n```\\n\\nI would fix `src/billing.ts` first by removing repeated wall-clock reads, then audit the date helper for the UTC/local mix because that is the most likely source of off-by-one date behavior across machines.\"}}\n{\"type\":\"turn.completed\",\"usage\":{\"input_tokens\":29264,\"cached_input_tokens\":12160,\"output_tokens\":533,\"reasoning_output_tokens\":74}}\n",
/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.9.4/claude/bugfix-01-messy-ts-app.json:7:  "response": "# Most likely cause\n\n**`crime_00004` — Half-UTC, Half-Local on the same `Date` receiver in `src/date.ts`** (with **`crime_00010` — Timezone Roulette** as the close-second contributor).\n\n`src/date.ts` reads from one `d` using `getUTCFullYear()` at L9 *and* `getMonth()` at L41. Those two families disagree by the host's UTC offset, so any moment within `tzOffset` of midnight reports a year/month from a different calendar day depending on where the test runs. That is the textbook shape of a date-boundary flake: green on a dev laptop in one zone, red in CI in another, red only on certain days of the year.\n\nThe parsing finding compounds it: `new Date(\"2026-12-25T07:00:00\")` and `new Date(\"2026-12-20\")` (crime_00010, L29/L33) have **no zone marker**. `YYYY-MM-DD` is parsed as UTC, but `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS` (no `Z`) is parsed as **local** — so those two literals already live on different calendars before any arithmetic. Feed either of those into the mixed-family read at L9/L41 and you get exactly the year-end / month-end boundary failure billing tests would hit on 2026-12-20 vs 2026-12-25.\n\n`crime_00014`'s `+ 86400000` day math in the same file is the third leg — it drifts by an hour across DST transitions, which is also a plausible billing-boundary trigger but less likely to manifest in December than the UTC/local split.\n\n# Proposed fix\n\nIn `src/date.ts`:\n\n1. **Pick one family per receiver.** Convert every read on `d` to the UTC family (`getUTCMonth()` at L41, etc.), or to the local family — don't mix. Storage/comparison code wants UTC; only the display boundary should touch local.\n2. **Make the literals explicit.** Change `\"2026-12-25T07:00:00\"` → `\"2026-12-25T07:00:00Z\"` (or `+HH:MM`) and `\"2026-12-20\"` → `\"2026-12-20T00:00:00Z\"`. The bare-date form is already UTC, but writing it explicitly removes the ambiguity that's misleading whoever wrote the timestamped sibling.\n3. **Replace `+ 86400000` (L52) with a calendar-aware `addDays`** (Luxon / Temporal / date-fns-tz) so DST days don't silently lose or gain an hour.\n\nFor the test flake specifically, fix #1 alone should stop the boundary failure; #2 prevents it from coming back the next time someone adds a literal.\n\n# Why not the other high-severity findings\n\n- `crime_00002` (God Function `generateInvoice`, 204 lines) is real risk but structural — wouldn't cause a *date-boundary* failure on its own.\n- `crime_00003` / `crime_00009` (direct `Date.now()` / `new Date()` in billing & date.ts) make tests *time-dependent* but typically produce uniformly-flaky or always-failing tests, not specifically boundary failures. They become load-bearing only after #1 above is fixed and you want the tests to be reproducible — inject a clock then.",

/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.9.4/claude/bugfix-08-stress-dependency.json:7:  "response": "## The cycle\n\n```\napplication/billing-flow.ts → domain/billing.ts → presentation/invoice-view.ts → application/billing-flow.ts\n```\n\n## Break this edge: `domain/billing.ts → presentation/invoice-view.ts`\n\nThat single edge is doing double damage — it's both the import that closes the cycle *and* the one flagged as a layer violation (`domain cannotImport presentation`). Cutting it fixes both findings in one move and restores the intended direction of change (presentation → application → domain).\n\n**Why this edge and not the others:**\n- `application → domain` is the natural direction; domain shouldn't be reaching back into anything else.\n- `presentation → application` is also expected — UIs orchestrating use cases is normal.\n- `domain → presentation` is the only edge that inverts the layering. Domain code referencing a view means the view's shape has leaked into the core business model.\n\n**How to break it:**\n1. **Preferred:** identify what `billing.ts` is actually using from `invoice-view.ts`. If it's a type/shape (e.g. `InvoiceViewModel`), define that shape in `domain/` and have the presentation file import *it*, not the reverse. Domain owns the contract; presentation conforms.\n2. **If it's only types**, the cheapest mechanical fix is `import type { ... } from '../presentation/invoice-view'` — TS erases it at runtime so the cycle dissolves. Use this only as a stopgap; it preserves the layer violation conceptually even though the runtime edge is gone, and `crimes` will (correctly) still flag the layering finding.\n3. **If it's runtime behavior** (a function/class), invert via a domain-owned interface that presentation implements and inject at the application layer. This is the real fix.\n\nThe deep-import finding in `billing-flow.ts` is unrelated to the cycle — worth fixing separately but not blocking.",
/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.15/claude/context-01-messy-ts-app.json:7:  "response": "## What `src/billing.ts` does\n\nA 310-line billing module with 5 top-level functions. Two named entry points are visible in the findings:\n\n- `generateInvoice` (lines 50–253) — the bulk of the file, builds invoices and reads the system clock at 7 points (creation timestamps, due dates, etc.).\n- `calculateRefundPreview` (lines 40–44) — name suggests a pure calculation, but the body calls `saveRefundAudit` and `sendRefundEmail` (i.e. it commits a refund as a side effect).\n\nPlan/tier logic centres on the `\"enterprise\"` literal, which is duplicated across `src/api/plan.ts`, `src/jobs/plan-sync.ts`, and `src/ui/pricing.ts`. Line 39 carries an authorization rule in a comment that isn't obviously encoded in code: *\"Only owners can refund enterprise plans unless billing support approves.\"*\n\n## Files to read before editing\n\n| File | Why |\n|---|---|\n| `src/billing.test.ts` | Two existing tests are flagged as weak — `\"renders the enterprise plan\"` has zero assertions, `\"creates an invoice\"` only uses weak matchers. Don't trust them as a safety net. |\n| `src/date.ts` | Billing leans on dates and `date.ts` itself has Temporal Recklessness (8 clock reads), a mixed UTC/local receiver, timezone-unsafe parses, and DST-naive ms math. Any date helper you reach for here is suspect. |\n| `src/api/plan.ts`, `src/jobs/plan-sync.ts`, `src/ui/pricing.ts` | Co-owners of the `\"enterprise\"` literal — a policy change here likely needs matching changes there. |\n| `docs/billing.md` | Concept alias drift: the \"tenant\" concept appears as account/organisation/team/workspace across docs and routes; check which term billing speaks. |\n| Callees of `calculateRefundPreview` | `saveRefundAudit`, `sendRefundEmail` (not in this file). Worth opening to understand the hidden side effects before refactoring. |\n\n## Risks the scan flagged on this file\n\n**High**\n- **God Function** — `generateInvoice` is 204 lines, 3.4× the 60-line threshold. /Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.9.4/claude/refactor-01-messy-ts-app.json:7:  "response": "# Top 3 risk-reduction changes for `src/billing.ts`\n\n## 1. Split `generateInvoice` into named helpers (also defangs the God File)\n- **Findings**: `crime_00002` (God Function, **high**, lines 50–253, 204 lines / 3.4× threshold) and `crime_00016` (God File, medium, 310 lines).\n- **Why**: `generateInvoice` is the dominant mass in the file — `crime_00016` only crosses the 300-line threshold *because* `generateInvoice` does. Five of the seven `Date` calls flagged by `crime_00009` (L57, L63, L92, L198, L238, L250) also live inside it, so right now any change to one phase of invoicing has to be reasoned about against ~200 lines of unrelated logic.\n- **Move**: extract cohesive sections — proration, line-item assembly, tax/total roll-up, due-date computation, persistence/notifications — into named helpers. Same module is fine for the first pass; the goal is local readability and isolated testability, not a new package layout. This single change collapses two findings and dramatically narrows the blast radius of every future edit.\n\n## 2. Rename `calculateRefundPreview` and encode the comment rule above it\n- **Findings**: `crime_00013` (False Identity, medium, agent_risk 0.44 — `calculateRefundPreview` calls `saveRefundAudit` and `sendRefundEmail`) and `crime_00017` (Logic in the Alibi, medium, line 39 — *\"Only owners can refund enterprise plans unless billing support approves.\"*).\n- **Why**: this is the highest-agent-risk pairing in the file. The name says \"Preview\" — i.e. pure — but the body emails customers and writes audit rows. Worse, the load-bearing authorization rule lives in a comment one line above it, where neither the type system nor a test can enforce it. An agent that trusts the name and ignores the prose can ship an unauthorized refund *and* send mail.\n- **Move**: rename to something like `previewAndIssueRefund` (or extract the pure preview math out of the side-effecting flow), then convert the comment into a named guard such as `assertCanRefund({ actor, plan })` that throws on the owner / enterprise / billing-support edge. Now the rule shows up in callers, tests, and type checks instead of relying on prose discipline.\n\n## 3. Inject a clock for the seven `Date` reads\n- **Finding**: `crime_00009` (Temporal Recklessness, medium, 7 calls at L57, L63, L92, L198, L238, L250, L273).\n- **Why**: billing logic is exactly where non-determinism is most damaging — invoice timestamps, due dates, proration cutoffs, and trial-end checks all change with wall time, and the existing test surface is already weak (`crime_00011` and `crime_00030` flag two billing tests as proving nothing). Until `now()` is injectable, no test can pin \"what does this invoice look like at 2026-12-31T23:59:00Z?\", so refactors #1 and #2 ship without a safety net.\n- **Move**: add a `clock: () => Date` (or `now: () => number`) dependency on the billing module's entry points and thread it through. Default to `() => new Date()` in production wiring, pass a fixed instant in tests. Cheapest of the three changes, and it unlocks meaningful assertions on the other two.\n\n**Note**: the prompt suggested running `crimes context src/billing.ts` for symbol/import surface; I only had the scan JSON to work from, so the ranking above is based on severity × agent_risk × overlap-with-other-findings rather than the import graph. If callers of `calculateRefundPreview` are widespread, item #2 jumps to #1.",
/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.15/claude/refactor-01-plural-mismatch.json:7:  "response": "The scan turned up **one** `singular_plural_type_mismatch` finding (`crime_00034`) covering two declarations in `src/api/state.ts`. Both are unambiguous because the file already includes correctly-named negative controls (`user: User` @L33, `invoices: Invoice[]` @L34) — those tell us which side is load-bearing in each case.\n\n---\n\n### 1. `users: User` @L24\n\nEvidence (verbatim):\n> `plural name, singular type: ``users: User`` @L24`\n\n```ts\nexport const users: User = { id: \"u_1\", email: \"a@b.c\" };\n```\n\nThe initializer is a single object literal (`{ id, email }`), not an array — so the **type carries the intended meaning**. The variable holds one user; the plural name is the lie.\n\n**Proposal (a) — rename to match the type:**\n```ts\nexport const user1: User = { id: \"u_1\", email: \"a@b.c\" };\n// or simply merge with the existing `user` const on L33\n```\nA bare `user` would collide with line 33, which is why I'd suggest a disambiguating suffix or just removing this declaration if the L33 one is canonical.\n\nProposal (b) — change the type to match the name — would require fabricating a second element from nothing and rewriting the initializer. The shape of the value is the source of truth here, so go with (a).\n\n---\n\n### 2. `invoice: Invoice[]` @L27\n\nEvidence (verbatim):\n> `singular name, array type: ``invoice: Invoice[]`` @L27`\n\n```ts\nexport const invoice: Invoice[] = [];\n```\n\nThe annotation is `Invoice[]` and the initializer is `[]` — the array shape is committed to on both the type and the value side. The **type carries the intended meaning**; the singular name is the lie. The negative control on L34 (`export const invoices: Invoice[] = []`) confirms `invoices` is the project's chosen name for this shape.\n\n**Proposal (a) — rename to match the type:**\n```ts\nexport const invoices: Invoice[] = [];\n// collides with L34 — delete one, since they're identical\n```\n\nProposal (b) — collapsing `Invoice[]` to `Invoice` — would force `= []` to become a single `Invoice` object, which contradicts both the empty-array initializer and the existence of the `invoices` const right below it. Go with (a).\n\n---\n\n### Summary\n\n| Decl | Intended meaning lives in | Fix |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| `users: User` @L24 | the type (single user, object initializer) | rename → singular (`user`, deduped against L33) |\n| `invoice: Invoice[]` @L27 | the type (array, `[]` initializer, mirrors L34) | rename → plural (`invoices`, deduped against L34) |\n\nIn both cases the **rename** is the right move — the value shape and the adjacent negative controls make the type the source of truth.",
/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.15/claude/refactor-08-architecture.json:7:  "response": "## Findings to address\n\n**crime_00002 — layer_violation** (`severity: medium`, `agent_risk: 0.63`)\n`src/domain/billing.ts` → `src/presentation/invoice-view.ts`. Violates `domain cannotImport presentation`.\n\n**crime_00003 — deep_import** (`severity: low`, `agent_risk: 0.52`)\n`src/application/billing-flow.ts` → `some-pkg/dist/internal/private/helper.js`.\n\n(Both are entangled with crime_00001 — the layer_violation edge is one of the three sides of the import cycle.)\n\n## Proposed changes\n\n### 1. Break: `domain/billing.ts` → `presentation/invoice-view.ts` (ties to crime_00002)\n\nThe domain layer must not know how an invoice is rendered. Two cases, pick by what's actually being imported:\n\n- **If the import is a type only** → convert to `import type { ... } from \"../presentation/invoice-view\"` so it carries no runtime edge. Cheap, but a code smell — the type probably belongs in domain.\n- **If the import is a value (function/class)** → apply dependency inversion. Define the contract in domain:\n  ```ts\n  // src/domain/invoice-renderer.ts\n  export interface InvoiceRenderer { render(invoice: Invoice): string }\n  ```\n  `presentation/invoice-view.ts` implements it; `application/billing-flow.ts` wires the concrete implementation into the domain caller. Domain now points outward only through an interface it owns.\n\nThis single change also collapses the cycle in crime_00001, because the cycle's domain→presentation edge is the same edge.\n\n### 2. Redirect: `application/billing-flow.ts` deep import (ties to crime_00003)\n\nReplace `some-pkg/dist/internal/private/helper.js` with `some-pkg`'s public entry:\n\n```ts\nimport { helper } from \"some-pkg\"   // if exported\n```\n\nIf the symbol isn't on the public surface, the suggested action is right: file an upstream issue rather than locking us to a private path. Do **not** wrap the deep import in a local re-export — that just hides the same coupling behind one more file.\n\n## How the two findings differ in agent risk\n\nBoth score `test_gap: 1` and identical `blast_radius`, so the gap between 0.63 and 0.52 is almost entirely **severity** — and severity here is shorthand for *who gets misled and how*.\n\n- **Layer violation is a directional invariant inside our code.** An agent reading `domain/billing.ts` and seeing a presentation import will reasonably conclude that \"domain depends on presentation\" is allowed, and will propagate that pattern on the next change. It inverts the intended direction of change for the whole codebase, not just this file. That's why it scores higher despite being \"only\" medium severity.\n- **Deep import is a coupling to code we don't own.** The blast radius inside *our* repo is small — one file, one import — and an agent editing this file isn't going to mis-learn an architectural rule from it. The real risk is silent breakage on a `some-pkg` upgrade, which is a temporal/maintenance risk, not a confusion-propagation risk. Lower agent risk, correctly.\n\nShort version: layer_violation teaches the wrong lesson; deep_import is a tripwire that hasn't tripped yet.",

Break up `generateInvoice` (crime_00002, knocks down crime_00016)\n\n204 lines spanning L50–253 — 3.4× the domain threshold and the single highest-severity finding in the file (severity 0.9). It's also the reason the file as a whole trips God File at 310 lines; carve cohesive sections out of `generateInvoice` and the file shrinks below the 300-line line by construction. Extract along responsibility seams (line-item assembly, tax/proration, persistence, notification) so an agent editing one section can't silently break the others.\n\n## 2. Encode the L39 comment rule and rename `calculateRefundPreview` (crime_00017 + crime_00013)\n\nThese are the same bug wearing two hats. The comment at L39 — *\"Only owners can refund enterprise plans unless billing support approves\"* — is a policy the surrounding code doesn't visibly enforce, and the function it precedes (`calculateRefundPreview`, L40–44) lies about itself: a \"preview\" / \"calculate\" name that actually calls `saveRefundAudit` and `sendRefundEmail`. An agent reads the name, assumes pure, and ships an edit that double-charges audit rows or fires a second email. Fix by (a) extracting the role/plan check into a named guard (`assertCanRefundEnterprise(actor, plan)`) so the rule lives in code, and (b) splitting the function into `buildRefundPreview` (pure) + `commitRefund` (the audit + email side effects). This also gives the still-empty tests in `src/billing.test.ts` (crime_00011) something real to assert against.\n\n## 3. Inject a clock for the 7 `Date` calls in billing (crime_00009)\n\nSeven direct `Date.now()` / `new Date()` calls across L57, 63, 92, 198, 238, 250, 273. Invoice generation that reads wall-time is what's keeping the existing tests weak — \"renders the enterprise plan\" asserts nothing (crime_00011) and \"creates an invoice\" only uses a weak matcher (crime_00030) because there's no way to pin a stable `issuedAt`/`dueAt` without monkey-patching globals. Thread a `clock: () => Date` (or `now: () => number`) parameter through `generateInvoice` and its new helpers from step 1; default it to `Date.now` at the CLI/API boundary. Once invoice timestamps are deterministic, the tests can assert exact totals, due dates, and proration without flake — and the shared `src/date.ts` cluster (crime_00003, _00004, _00010, _00014) becomes the next obvious thing to clean up using the same clock seam.",

The singular name is the typo — the type carries the intended meaning.\n\n**Fix (a) — rename to match the type:**\n```ts\nexport const invoices: Invoice[] = [];\n```\nThat collides with line 34, so it really means the line-27 export is redundant and should be deleted, or renamed to something like `pendingInvoices` if it's meant to be a distinct list.\n\nI'd reject fix (b) (`invoice: Invoice = …`) for the same reason as above: the initializer `[]` is array-shaped, and there's no single-record meaning that `invoice` could legitimately carry given that `Invoice` requires `id` and `totalCents`.\n\n## Summary\n\nBoth declarations: **rename, don't reshape.** The types are correct and consistent with the file's own negative controls (lines 33–34); the names are off-by-one-letter typos. After fixing the name collisions with lines 33/34, the file should keep `User` for \"one user\" and `Invoice[]` for \"a list of invoices.\"",

A `<div>` subtree appears in 4 files with the same structural shape.\n\n## Files that would change\n\nThe shared subtree is the **form action bar** (Cancel link + primary submit button) at the bottom of each invoice form:\n\n- `dashboard/final-example/app/ui/invoices/create-form.tsx:142-150` (`Create Invoice`)\n- `dashboard/final-example/app/ui/invoices/edit-form.tsx:152-160` (`Edit Invoice`)\n- `dashboard/starter-example/app/ui/invoices/create-form.tsx:101-109`\n- `dashboard/starter-example/app/ui/invoices/edit-form.tsx:112-120`\n\nBoth starter and final variants would adopt the new component. (`final-example` and `starter-example` are separate Next.js apps in the fixture — each gets its own copy of the shared component unless the two apps share a package, which they don't here.)\n\nNew file per app:\n\n- `dashboard/final-example/app/ui/invoices/form-actions.tsx`\n- `dashboard/starter-example/app/ui/invoices/form-actions.tsx`\n\n## Proposed shared component\n\n```tsx\n// app/ui/invoices/form-actions.tsx\nimport Link from 'next/link';\nimport { Button } from '@/app/ui/button';\nimport type { ReactNode } from 'react';\n\ntype FormActionsProps = {\n  cancelHref: string;\n  submitLabel: ReactNode;     // \"Create Invoice\" | \"Edit Invoice\" | …\n  cancelLabel?: ReactNode;    // default: \"Cancel\"\n  submitDisabled?: boolean;\n  className?: string;         // override the wrapper utility classes\n  children?: ReactNode;       // optional slot: extra actions rendered BEFORE Cancel\n};\n\nexport function FormActions({\n  cancelHref,\n  submitLabel,\n  cancelLabel = 'Cancel',\n  submitDisabled,\n  className = 'mt-6 flex justify-end gap-4',\n  children,\n}: FormActionsProps) {\n  return (\n    <div className={className}>\n      {children}\n      <Link\n        href={cancelHref}\n        className=\"flex h-10 items-center rounded-lg bg-gray-100 px-4 text-sm font-medium text-gray-600 transition-colors hover:bg-gray-200\"\n      >\n        {cancelLabel}\n      </Link>\n      <Button type=\"submit\" disabled={submitDisabled}>\n        {submitLabel}\n      </Button>\n    </div>\n  );\n}\n```\n\n### Public API\n\n**Props**\n| Prop | Type | Purpose |\n|---|---|---|\n| `cancelHref` | `string` | Required. Where Cancel routes (`/dashboard/invoices` in both call sites today). |\n| `submitLabel` | `ReactNode` | Required. The varying bit — `\"Create Invoice\"` vs `\"Edit Invoice\"`. |\n| `cancelLabel` | `ReactNode` | Optional, defaults to `\"Cancel\"`. Lets future forms localize. |\n| `submitDisabled` | `boolean` | Optional. Lets callers wire `useFormStatus().pending` without re-templating. |\n| `className` | `string` | Optional escape hatch for the wrapper utilities. |\n\n**Slots**\n- `children` — rendered **before** Cancel, for forms that grow a third action (e.g. a \"Save draft\" or \"Delete\" button). Keep it as a single optional slot rather than named `before`/`after` props until a second use case appears.\n\n### Call-site change (representative)\n\n```tsx\n// create-form.tsx — replaces lines 142–150\n<FormActions cancelHref=\"/dashboard/invoices\" submitLabel=\"Create Invoice\" />\n\n// edit-form.tsx — replaces lines 152–160\n<FormActions cancelHref=\"/dashboard/invoices\" submitLabel=\"Edit Invoice\" />\n```\n\n### Notes\n\n- This is a JSX-shape consolidation, not a behavior change — the existing `<form>`, fieldset, and `aria-live` region around it stay put.\n- `final-example` and `starter-example` are separate apps; each needs its own `FormActions` file unless you also introduce a shared package. The shape (props + slot) is identical across both copies, so future drift between them will surface as a `duplicate_component_shape` finding on the new file too — that's the desired signal.\n- Related but **not** addressed by this component: `crime_00053`/`crime_00054` (status string checks scattered across forms + `status.tsx`) and `crime_00051` (`\"status\"` literal repeated in 5 files). /Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.15/claude/refactor-01-large-file.json:7:  "response": "## Plan to split `src/billing.ts` — anchored to `large_file` (crime_00016)\n\n**The evidence**: 310 non-empty lines (1.0× the 300 threshold) and **5 top-level functions** — those 5 functions are the natural unit of split, and three of them (`generateInvoice`, `tax`, `recommendUpgrade`) hide duplicated logic that the current file lets drift silently. Co-located findings pin the seams:\n\n| Evidence anchor | Tells us |\n|---|---|\n| `crime_00002` — `generateInvoice` is 204 lines (L50–253) | One function is **~65% of the file**; relocating it alone drops the parent under threshold. |\n| `crime_00013` + `crime_00017` — `calculateRefundPreview` does side-effects, and the **prose rule** at L39 (\"only owners can refund enterprise plans unless billing support approves\") sits orphaned above it | Refund is a distinct concern from invoice generation, and the policy currently lives in a comment that no code enforces. |\n| `crime_00009` — 7 `Date` calls scattered across L57, 63, 92, 198, 238, 250, 273 | Clock access threads through **multiple functions** — extracting per-function won't fix it; needs a shared seam. |\n| Duplicated tax table at L145–154 vs `tax()` at L257–268; duplicated `daysSince` loyalty math at L92 vs `recommendUpgrade` at L273 | These are the cohesion seams — pulling tax and pricing into their own modules forces the duplicate copies to converge. |\n\n### Proposed file layout\n\n```\nsrc/billing/\n  types.ts        // L4–31 — User, InvoiceLine, Invoice interfaces\n  refund.ts       // L40–44 calculateRefundPreview + the L39 rule encoded as a guard\n  tax.ts          // L257–268 tax() + the country→rate table currently duplicated at L147–154\n  pricing.ts      // L66–126: plan adjustments, loyalty discount, promo resolution, currency\n  risk.ts         // L128–143 fraud-risk scoring\n  invoice.ts      // L50–253 generateInvoice — now an orchestrator that calls pricing/tax/risk\n  upgrade.ts      // L272–305 recommendUpgrade\n  index.ts        // barrel re-exporting the public surface\n```\n\n(`listRecentInvoices` at L307–310 is a TODO stub — keep it in `invoice.ts` or delete; not worth its own file.)\n\n### Order of operations (each step lands under green tests)\n\n1. **`types.ts` first.** Pure move of L4–31. No behaviour change. Everything else will import from it.\n2. **`tax.ts` second.** Move L257–268. *Then* replace the duplicated table at L145–154 inside `generateInvoice` with `tax(user.country, afterDiscount)`. This is the smallest split that erases a duplicate (`crime_00016` evidence: 5 top-level fns → 4 after, and the duplicate vanishes).\n3. **`refund.ts` third.** Move `calculateRefundPreview` *and* convert the L39 comment into a guard (`canRefund(user, invoice)` — addresses `crime_00017`). Rename to disclose the side effects per `crime_00013` (e.g. `previewAndRecordRefund` or split pure preview from `recordRefundAudit`/`sendRefundEmail`).\n4. **`pricing.ts` + `risk.ts`.** Extract the labelled sections inside `generateInvoice` (the L66 / L91 / L105 / L128 comment-banners are already the section boundaries the original author drew). `generateInvoice` becomes the orchestrator. This is where the `large_function` charge (`crime_00002`) actually resolves — but it's a separate refactor, scheduled here because once it's out of `billing.ts` the parent finding drops well below threshold without touching it.\n5. **`upgrade.ts`.** Move `recommendUpgrade`. While moving it, replace its inline `daysSince` math (L273) with a shared `daysSince(user)` helper alongside the loyalty math in `pricing.ts` — the duplicate `(Date.now() - user.createdAt) / (1000*60*60*24)` at L92 and L273 was a smell that a same-file reader rarely sees.\n6. **`index.ts` barrel.** Re-export the public surface — but only the symbols actually imported elsewhere.\n\n### Callers to update\n\n`grep -rn \"from.*billing\"` across `src/` returns **zero hits** — nothing in the repo imports `src/billing.ts` today. `src/billing.test.ts` uses local stubs (`renderEnterprisePlan`, `createInvoice`), not the real exports (and is the subject of `crime_00011` / `crime_00030` weak-test findings — worth fixing in the same PR but not blocking the split).\n\nSo caller churn is essentially:\n\n- **External imports**: none to update.\n- **Internal imports within the new `src/billing/` tree**: `invoice.ts` will import from `./pricing`, `./tax`, `./risk`, `./types`; `refund.ts` and `upgrade.ts` import from `./types`.\n- **`src/billing.test.ts`**: should be rewritten to actually call the new modules (separate concern, but the split makes that possible — right now the test stubs avoid the file because the file is too tangled to test).\n\n### What this plan deliberately does **not** do\n\n- Doesn't touch `crime_00009` (clock injection) as part of the split — that's a cross-cutting change that should land *after* the modules exist, so the clock seam can be added in one place each (`pricing.ts`, `invoice.ts`, `upgrade.ts`) rather than threaded through one 310-line file. Splits first; clock seam second.\n- Doesn't try to fix `generateInvoice`'s internal God-Function structure (`crime_00002`) in the same PR — step 4 *enables* that work by giving it a target module, but extracting helpers inside `invoice.ts` is a separate, reviewable change.\n- Doesn't centralise the `\"enterprise\"` literal (`crime_00012`) yet — that's a cross-file fix; this PR only splits one file.",
/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.15/claude/bugfix-01-messy-ts-app.json:7:  "response": "# Most likely cause\n\n**`crime_00004` — Half-UTC, Half-Local in `src/date.ts`** is the smoking gun. The same `d` receiver is read with `getUTCFullYear()` at L9 and `getMonth()` (local) at L41. Those two families disagree by the host's UTC offset on exactly the boundary cases that flake — late-evening or early-morning instants where the UTC date and local date differ.\n\nTwo related findings amplify it:\n\n- **`crime_00010` — Timezone Roulette** at `src/date.ts:29,33`: `new Date(\"2026-12-25T07:00:00\")` and `new Date(\"2026-12-20\")` parse with no zone. The first uses host-local time; the second uses UTC. So the *same literal-looking code path* yields different absolute instants depending on which constructor branch runs, and the resulting `Date` is then fed to the mixed UTC/local readers above.\n- **`crime_00014` — DST-Naive Day Math** at `src/date.ts:52`: `+ 86400000` for \"one day\". On DST-transition days the result is off by an hour, which is enough to flip the local date when the original instant is near midnight.\n\n`crime_00009` (7 direct `Date.now()`/`new Date()` in `billing.ts`) is why the failure is *flaky* rather than constant — wall time supplies the boundary instant that exposes the bug.\n\n# Proposed fix\n\nOrder by leverage, smallest blast radius first:\n\n1. **`src/date.ts:9 & 41` — unify the family on `d`.** Pick UTC throughout the function (it's already using `getUTCFullYear`) and change `getMonth()` → `getUTCMonth()`. This alone should stabilize the boundary tests if the bug is the local/UTC split on a single receiver.\n2. **`src/date.ts:29,33` — make the literals explicit.** Append `Z` (or a `±HH:MM` offset matching the business intent) to `\"2026-12-25T07:00:00\"` and `\"2026-12-20\"`. Document which one is meant.\n3. **`src/date.ts:52` — replace `+ 86400000` with a calendar-aware `addDays`** (Temporal, Luxon, or date-fns-tz). Required for any boundary that crosses a DST shift.\n4. **Then** address the temporal coupling: inject a `Clock` / `now()` into `generateInvoice` (`crime_00009`) so the test can pin the instant and the bug becomes reproducible instead of flaky. This is the real long-term fix — without it you're patching symptoms.\n\nThe minimal change that should turn the test green is step 1. Steps 2–3 close adjacent boundary bugs the same test set is likely to hit next. Step 4 is the durable fix.",
